title: I
html:
I

If it had not been for those two mettlesome tits, and that madcap of a postillion who drove them from Stilton to Stamford, the thought had never entered my head. He flew like lightningпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthere was a slope of three miles and a halfпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњwe scarce touched the groundпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthe motion was most rapidпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњmost impetuousпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФвАЩtwas communicated to my brainпїњвАФmy heart partook of itпїњвАФпїњвАФвАЬBy the great God of day,вАЭ said I, looking towards the sun, and thrusting my arm out of the fore-window of the chaise, as I made my vow, вАЬI will lock up my study-door the moment I get home, and throw the key of it ninety feet below the surface of the earth, into the draw-well at the back of my house.вАЭ

The London wagon confirmed me in my resolution; it hung tottering upon the hill, scarce progressive, dragвАЩdпїњвАФdragвАЩd up by eight heavy beastsпїњвАФвАЬby main strength!пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњquoth I, noddingпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњbut your betters draw the same wayпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand something of everybodyвАЩs!пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњO rare!вАЭ

Tell me, ye learned, shall we forever be adding so much to the bulkпїњвАФso little to the stock?

Shall we forever make new books, as apothecaries make new mixtures, by pouring only out of one vessel into another?

Are we forever to be twisting, and untwisting the same rope? forever in the same trackпїњвАФforever at the same pace?

Shall we be destined to the days of eternity, on holy-days, as well as working-days, to be showing the relicks of learning, as monks do the relicks of their saintsпїњвАФwithout working oneпїњвАФone single miracle with them?

Who made Man, with powers which dart him from earth to heaven in a momentпїњвАФthat great, that most excellent, and most noble creature of the worldпїњвАФthe miracle of nature, as Zoroaster in his book ѕАќµѕБќє ѕЖѕНѕГќµѕЙѕВ called himпїњвАФthe Shekinah of the divine presence, as ChrysostomпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthe image of God, as MosesпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthe ray of divinity, as PlatoпїњвАФthe marvel of marvels, as AristotleпїњвАФto go sneaking on at this pitifulпїњвАФpimpingпїњвАФpettifogging rate?

I scorn to be as abusive as Horace upon the occasionпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФbut if there is no catachresis in the wish, and no sin in it, I wish from my soul, that every imitator in Great Britain, France, and Ireland, had the farcy for his pains; and that there was a good farcical house, large enough to holdпїњвАФayeпїњвАФand sublimate them, shag rag and bobtail, male and female, all together: and this leads me to the affair of WhiskersпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњbut, by what chain of ideasпїњвАФI leave as a legacy in mortmain to Prudes and Tartufs, to enjoy and make the most of.

Upon Whiskers

IвАЩm sorry I made itпїњвАФпїњвАФвАЩtwas as inconsiderate a promise as ever entered a manвАЩs headпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњA chapter upon whiskers! alas! the world will not bear itпїњвАФвАЩtis a delicate worldпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњbut I knew not of what mettle it was madeпїњвАФnor had I ever seen the underwritten fragment; otherwise, as surely as noses are noses, and whiskers are whiskers still (let the world say what it will to the contrary); so surely would I have steered clear of this dangerous chapter.

The Fragment

* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФYou are half asleep, my good lady, said the old gentleman, taking hold of the old ladyвАЩs hand, and giving it a gentle squeeze, as he pronounced the word WhiskersпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњshall we change the subject? By no means, replied the old ladyпїњвАФI like your account of those matters; so throwing a thin gauze handkerchief over her head, and leaning it back upon the chair with her face turned towards him, and advancing her two feet as she reclined herselfпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњI desire, continued she, you will go on.

The old gentleman went on as follows:пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФWhiskers! cried the queen of Navarre, dropping her knotting ball, as La Fosseuse uttered the wordпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњWhiskers, madam, said La Fosseuse, pinning the ball to the queenвАЩs apron, and making a courtesy as she repeated it.

La FosseuseвАЩs voice was naturally soft and low, yet вАЩtwas an articulate voice: and every letter of the word Whiskers fell distinctly upon the queen of NavarreвАЩs earпїњвАФWhiskers! cried the queen, laying a greater stress upon the word, and as if she had still distrusted her earsпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњWhiskers! replied La Fosseuse, repeating the word a third timeпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњThere is not a cavalier, madam, of his age in Navarre, continued the maid of honour, pressing the pageвАЩs interest upon the queen, that has so gallant a pairпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњOf what? cried Margaret, smilingпїњвАФOf whiskers, said La Fosseuse, with infinite modesty.

The word Whiskers still stood its ground, and continued to be made use of in most of the best companies throughout the little kingdom of Navarre, notwithstanding the indiscreet use which La Fosseuse had made of it: the truth was, La Fosseuse had pronounced the word, not only before the queen, but upon sundry other occasions at court, with an accent which always implied something of a mysteryпїњвАФAnd as the court of Margaret, as all the world knows, was at that time a mixture of gallantry and devotionпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand whiskers being as applicable to the one, as the other, the word naturally stood its groundпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњit gainвАЩd full as much as it lost; that is, the clergy were for itпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthe laity were against itпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand for the women,пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthey were divided.

The excellency of the figure and mien of the young Sieur De Croix, was at that time beginning to draw the attention of the maids of honour towards the terrace before the palace gate, where the guard was mounted. The lady De Baussiere fell deeply in love with him,пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњLa Battarelle did the sameпїњвАФit was the finest weather for it, that ever was remembered in NavarreпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњLa Guyol, La Maronette, La Sabatiere, fell in love with the Sieur De Croix alsoпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњLa Rebours and La Fosseuse knew betterпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњDe Croix had failed in an attempt to recommend himself to La Rebours; and La Rebours and La Fosseuse were inseparable.

The queen of Navarre was sitting with her ladies in the painted bow-window, facing the gate of the second court, as De Croix passed through itпїњвАФHe is handsome, said the Lady Baussiere.пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњHe has a good mien, said La BattarelleпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњHe is finely shaped, said La GuyolпїњвАФI never saw an officer of the horse-guards in my life, said La Maronette, with two such legsпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњOr who stood so well upon them, said La SabatiereпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФBut he has no whiskers, cried La FosseuseпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњNot a pile, said La Rebours.

The queen went directly to her oratory, musing all the way, as she walked through the gallery, upon the subject; turning it this way and that way in her fancyпїњвАФAve Maria!пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФwhat can La Fosseuse mean? said she, kneeling down upon the cushion.

La Guyol, La Battarelle, La Maronette, La Sabatiere, retired instantly to their chambersпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФWhiskers! said all four of them to themselves, as they bolted their doors on the inside.

The Lady Carnavallette was counting her beads with both hands, unsuspected, under her farthingalпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњfrom St.¬†Antony down to St.¬†Ursula inclusive, not a saint passed through her fingers without whiskers; St.¬†Francis, St.¬†Dominick, St.¬†Bennet, St.¬†Basil, St.¬†Bridget, had all whiskers.

The Lady Baussiere had got into a wilderness of conceits, with moralizing too intricately upon La FosseuseвАЩs textпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњShe mounted her palfrey, her page followed herпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthe host passed byпїњвАФthe Lady Baussiere rode on.

One denier, cried the order of mercyпїњвАФone single denier, in behalf of a thousand patient captives, whose eyes look towards heaven and you for their redemption.

вАФпїњвАФпїњThe Lady Baussiere rode on.

Pity the unhappy, said a devout, venerable, hoary-headed man, meekly holding up a box, begirt with iron, in his withered handsпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњI beg for the unfortunateпїњвАФgood my Lady, вАЩtis for a prisonпїњвАФfor an hospitalпїњвАФвАЩtis for an old manпїњвАФa poor man undone by shipwreck, by suretyship, by fireпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњI call God and all his angels to witnessпїњвАФпїњвАФвАЩtis to clothe the nakedпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњto feed the hungryпїњвАФпїњвАФвАЩtis to comfort the sick and the brokenhearted.

The Lady Baussiere rode on.

A decayed kinsman bowed himself to the ground.

вАФпїњвАФпїњThe Lady Baussiere rode on.

He ran begging bareheaded on one side of her palfrey, conjuring her by the former bonds of friendship, alliance, consanguinity, etc.пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњCousin, aunt, sister, mother,пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњfor virtueвАЩs sake, for your own, for mine, for ChristвАЩs sake, remember meпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњpity me.

вАФпїњвАФпїњThe Lady Baussiere rode on.

Take hold of my whiskers, said the Lady BaussiereпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњThe page took hold of her palfrey. She dismounted at the end of the terrace.

There are some trains of certain ideas which leave prints of themselves about our eyes and eyebrows; and there is a consciousness of it, somewhere about the heart, which serves but to make these etchings the strongerпїњвАФwe see, spell, and put them together without a dictionary.

Ha, ha! he, hee! cried La Guyol and La Sabatiere, looking close at each otherвАЩs printsпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњHo, ho! cried La Battarelle and Maronette, doing the same:пїњвАФWhist! cried oneпїњвАФst, st,пїњвАФsaid a secondпїњвАФhush, quoth a thirdпїњвАФpoo, poo, replied a fourthпїњвАФgramercy! cried the Lady Carnavallette;пїњвАФпїњвАФвАЩtwas she who bewhiskerвАЩd St.¬†Bridget.

La Fosseuse drew her bodkin from the knot of her hair, and having traced the outline of a small whisker, with the blunt end of it, upon one side of her upper lip, put it into La ReboursвАЩ handпїњвАФLa Rebours shook her head.

The Lady Baussiere coughed thrice into the inside of her muffпїњвАФLa Guyol smiledпїњвАФFy, said the Lady Baussiere. The queen of Navarre touched her eye with the tip of her forefingerпїњвАФas much as to say, I understand you all.

вАЩTwas plain to the whole court the word was ruined: La Fosseuse had given it a wound, and it was not the better for passing through all these defilesпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњIt made a faint stand, however, for a few months, by the expiration of which, the Sieur De Croix, finding it high time to leave Navarre for want of whiskersпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthe word in course became indecent, and (after a few efforts) absolutely unfit for use.

The best word, in the best language of the best world, must have suffered under such combinations.пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФThe curate of dвАЩEstella wrote a book against them, setting forth the dangers of accessory ideas, and warning the Navarois against them.

Does not all the world know, said the curate dвАЩEstella at the conclusion of his work, that Noses ran the same fate some centuries ago in most parts of Europe, which Whiskers have now done in the kingdom of Navarre?пїњвАФThe evil indeed spread no farther thenпїњвАФbut have not beds and bolsters, and nightcaps and chamberpots stood upon the brink of destruction ever since? Are not trouse, and placket-holes, and pump-handlesпїњвАФand spigots and faucets, in danger still from the same association?пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњChastity, by nature, the gentlest of all affectionsпїњвАФgive it but its headпїњвАФпїњвАФвАЩtis like a ramping and a roaring lion.

The drift of the curate dвАЩEstellaвАЩs argument was not understood.пїњвАФThey ran the scent the wrong way.пїњвАФThe world bridled his ass at the tail.пїњвАФAnd when the extremes of delicacy, and the beginnings of concupiscence, hold their next provincial chapter together, they may decree that bawdy also.
